title: Promise vs setTimeout — Execution Order
explanation: Synchronous code runs first (Start, End). Then the microtask queue is drained (Promise.then runs), and only after that does the macrotask (setTimeout callback) execute.

title: Nested Promises & setTimeout
explanation: 5 prints first (sync). Then microtask queue: first .then prints 2 and schedules setTimeout(3). Second .then prints 4. Then macrotask queue: first setTimeout prints 1, then setTimeout(3) prints 3.
mentalModel: Each .then() is a separate microtask, but they all drain before any macrotask

title: async/await vs Promise.then Order
output:
script start
foo start
script end
foo end
explanation: foo() is called — 'foo start' prints synchronously. await suspends foo, placing 'foo end' as a microtask. 'script end' runs. Then the microtask fires.
mentalModel: await = split point. Everything after await becomes a microtask

title: setTimeout Call Stack & Event Loop
explanation: setTimeout callback goes to the task queue. foo() and baz() run first on the call stack, printing 'First' and 'Third'. Only when the call stack is empty does the event loop push 'Second'.
mentalModel: setTimeout goes to the task queue. It only runs after the call stack is completely empty

title: async/await Returns a Pending Promise
explanation: An async function ALWAYS returns a promise. The data variable holds this pending promise, not the resolved value. To get 'I made it!', you'd need: data.then(res => console.log(res)).
mentalModel: async functions return promises, not values. Use .then() or await to unwrap

title: Promise.race — First to Resolve Wins
explanation: Promise.race resolves with the FIRST promise that settles. secondPromise (100ms) beats firstPromise (500ms).
mentalModel: Promise.race = first one to cross the finish line wins

title: await Pauses vs .then() Continues
output:
Last line!
Promise!
Promise!
Last line!
Timeout!
Timeout!
explanation: funcOne: .then() is non-blocking, so 'Last line!' prints first. funcTwo: await PAUSES execution. Both setTimeout callbacks run last as macrotasks.
mentalModel: .then() is non-blocking (code after it runs immediately). await is blocking within its async function

title: Promise.then Chaining — Return Values
explanation: Each .then() receives the return of the previous .then(). 5*2=10, 10+1=11.
mentalModel: Each .then() receives the return value of the previous .then() as its argument

title: Promise.all — One Rejection Kills All
explanation: Promise.all rejects immediately if ANY promise rejects. promise3 rejects with 'Third', so the whole Promise.all rejects and the .catch fires with 'Third'.
mentalModel: Promise.all = all or nothing. One rejection = entire batch fails

title: Complex Event Loop — funcOne and funcTwo Interleaving
output:
Last line 1!
I have resolved!
I have resolved!
Last line 2!
Timeout!
Timeout 2!
explanation: funcOne's .then is non-blocking → 'Last line 1!' prints first. funcTwo's await pauses it. Then microtask queue drains: funcOne's chained .then prints, then funcTwo resumes. Both setTimeouts run last.
mentalModel: Sync code → microtasks (interleaved from both functions) → macrotasks

title: try/catch/finally with await
output:
Woah some cool data
Oh finally!
explanation: The promise resolves successfully, so the try block logs the value. No error → catch is skipped. finally ALWAYS runs regardless.
mentalModel: finally runs no matter what — success or failure. It's your cleanup block

title: setInterval Returns an ID, Not a Value
explanation: setInterval returns a unique numeric ID that can be used with clearInterval() to stop the timer. The callback hasn't fired yet — only the ID is returned synchronously.
mentalModel: setInterval/setTimeout return IDs, not results. The callback runs later
